heading: Why Brackets Matter
body: **Parentheses** show which parts of a symbolic sentence belong together. They make the grouping visible, so the translation matches the English structure.

examples.text: "If it rains and it freezes, then the roads are dangerous" needs grouping inside the if-part.
"It rains and if it freezes, then the roads are dangerous" has a different grouping.

note: This lesson builds on the earlier idea of the main operator.

heading: Group Inner Parts
body: **Grouping** follows the structure marked by English punctuation and connective words. First find the smaller unit, then place parentheses around that unit before attaching it to the rest of the sentence.

examples.text: "If Alice goes and Bob goes, then Charlie stays" groups "Alice goes and Bob goes" as one part.

heading: Conditional Pattern
body: When a conditional contains a compound antecedent or consequent, bracket that compound part. Then place $\rightarrow$ between the two larger parts.

examples.text: "If it rains and it freezes, then the roads are dangerous" becomes $(R \wedge F) \rightarrow D$.
"If it rains and it freezes, then the roads are dangerous" becomes $R \wedge (F \rightarrow D)$.

heading: Bracketed Translations
body: Read the English sentence in parts. Then mirror those parts with parentheses.

examples.text: "If the lamp is on or the window is open, then the room is bright" becomes $(L \vee W) \rightarrow B$.
"If the key is lost, then the door is shut and the alarm is on" becomes $K \rightarrow (D \wedge A)$.
"If the key is lost, then the door is shut and the alarm is on" becomes $(K \rightarrow D) \wedge A$.

heading: Do Not Flatten
body: A common mistake is writing all operators in one line without brackets. That hides the hierarchy and can change the sentence's meaning.

examples.text: $(R \wedge F) \rightarrow D$ and $R \wedge (F \rightarrow D)$ do not say the same thing.
If two translations use the same letters and operators, they always mean the same thing.

note: Brackets are not decoration. They mark structure.

heading: Outer Operator Last
body: Find the main operator last. Everything it governs should appear as the largest bracketed parts of the symbolic translation.

examples.text: In $(R \wedge F) \rightarrow D$, the inner conjunction is grouped first, and the outer conditional governs the whole formula.

heading: Core Method
body: Use parentheses to mirror English grouping. Build the inner parts first, then connect the larger parts with the main operator.
